full_text:
Name: Priya Kulkarni | Roll: IT/ 2023/017 | Page 2
2.2(b) Three Components of Attitude
Definition: Attitude is a learned predisposition to respond positively
or negatively toward a person, object, or situation.
1. Cognitive Component (Thinking) -
Beliefs, thoughts or knowledge a person holds about something
Eg: 'Working in a team increases productivity.'
2. Affective Component (Feeling) -
Emotions or feelings towards something.
Eg: Feeling happy when assigned a challenging project.
3. Behavioural Component (Acting) -
Intent or observable actions based on belief's and feelings.
Eg: Actively participating in team discussions.
02(c) Theory X and Theory Y - Mixed Team Scenario
(i) Applying only Theory X:
Theory X assumes employees are lazy, avoid responsibility, need
constant supervision. Applied to whole team, the self-motivated
employees will feel demotivated, their initiative is stifled, and
overall productivity falls significantly.
(ii) Applying only Theory Y:
Theory Y assumes all employees are responsible and self-motivated.
Risk: employees needing guidance will face confusion, mistakes
increase, and discipline may break down in that group.
(iii) Balanced Approach - Situational Leadership:

2. 1(a) OB Model Analysis - MotoCorp
(i) OB Model Identified: Custodial Model
The Custodial Model is based on economic resources. Management focuses
on providing job security and benefits to employees. Workers depend on
the organisati on rather than on just authority.
(i) Ad vant ages:
Provides stable employment and job security to employees
Builds employee loyalty and reduces resistance to authority.
Disadvantages:
Employees becomep assive and dependent - low motivation results.
Innovation and creativity are suppressed due to lack of challenge.
(iii) Recommended Shift: Supportive Mod el
The Supportive Model uses leadership, motivation andp articipation.
It encourages employee involvement and creativity. Employees shift from
passive cooperation to active engagement and higher productivity.
2.1(b) Definition and Scope of Organizational Behaviour
Definition: OB is a field of study that investigates the impact of
individuals, groups, and structure on behaviour within organi sat ions,
with purpose of applying this knowledge to improve effectiveness.
Scope of OB:
1. Individual Behaviour - Studies personality, perception, motivation,
learning and attitudes. Eg: Understanding why an employeeis
highly motivated compared to others.
2. Group Behaviour - Group dynamics, leadership, teamwork,

structured_answers.answer_text:
2(b) Three Components of Attitude
Definition: Attitude is a learned predisposition to respond positively
or negatively toward a person, object, or situation.
Affective Component (Feeling) -
Emotions or feelings towards something.
Eg: Feeling happy when assigned a challenging project.

Cognitive Component (Thinking) -
Beliefs, thoughts or knowledge a person holds about something
Eg: 'Working in a team increases productivity.'

Behavioural Component (Acting) -
Intent or observable actions based on belief's and feelings.
Eg: Actively participating in team discussions.
02(c) Theory X and Theory Y - Mixed Team Scenario
(i) Applying only Theory X:
Theory X assumes employees are lazy, avoid responsibility, need
constant supervision. Applied to whole team, the self-motivated
employees will feel demotivated, their initiative is stifled, and
overall productivity falls significantly.
(ii) Applying only Theory Y:
Theory Y assumes all employees are responsible and self-motivated.
Risk: employees needing guidance will face confusion, mistakes
increase, and discipline may break down in that group.
(iii) Balanced Approach - Situational Leadership:

Q.2(b) Three Components of Attitude
Definition: Attitude is a learned predisposition to respond positively or negatively toward a person, object, or situation.
1. Cognitive Component (Thinking) - Beliefs, thoughts or knowledge a person holds about something. Eg: 'Working in a team increases productivity.'
2. Affective Component (Feeling) - Emotions or feelings towards something. Eg: Feeling happy when assigned a challenging project.
3. Behavioural Component (Acting) - Intent or observable actions based on beliefs and feelings. Eg: Actively participating in team discussions.
